quote: I saw several advertisements on my Instagram that said that there was, fiscal availability from government or Virginia programs, or Elon Musk was on one, for basically free installation of the solar panels. If that's not true, I'm not interested.
stewart_read: The customer is not raising a scam accusation — she is raising a misinformation anchor. She has seen Instagram ads using Elon Musk's image to promise free solar. Parker's response at 00:46 ('we do take care of everything up front for you, nothing out of your pocket') is directionally correct on the bill-swap concept but does not acknowledge the misinformation context or validate her skepticism before pivoting. The gap is the missing empathy beat: she was targeted by deceptive advertising and is now on the phone with a solar company. A brief acknowledgment ('those ads are misleading and I understand why you'd be skeptical') before the reframe would have landed the bill-swap concept with more trust.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker's quick pivot to the bill-swap concept was strategic — get past the ad framing fast and onto real value. Possible, but the customer's next three turns all re-probe legitimacy, suggesting the trust gap was not closed.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the three-touch legitimacy pattern (00:39, 01:20, 02:54). Ask him: what would have changed if he had acknowledged the deceptive-ad context for five seconds before the reframe? The codex's scam_encounter_reference protocol (TBD) is the right home for this — flag for Kenny to populate with Ion-specific phrasing.

quote: So but you're charging people for the solar power?
stewart_read: This is the clearest flip opportunity in the call. The customer's frame is: 'you are charging me for something.' The inversion is: 'you are already being charged — by Dominion — and that charge goes up every year.' Parker instead re-explained the payment structure ('the pro how long ago do they get solar... the programs have changed'), which is a defensive answer that stays inside her frame rather than breaking it. The flip was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged that the customer needed factual grounding before an emotional flip would land. Possible, but the factual explanation at 01:25–02:03 did not resolve the concern — she asked the savings percentage question at 02:54, which is the same underlying probe.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to introduce the objection_inversion technique. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'You're already being charged for power — that's what your Dominion bill is. The question is whether you want to keep paying Dominion or redirect that payment to something you own.' No fabricated numbers needed. The flip works on the emotional logic, not the math.

quote: Most programs that I know of don't sell back or buy back any electric. It's more of just credits these days. Whereas a couple years ago, maybe five years ago, they used to buy it back a lot. It's just kinda been a changing program since then.
stewart_read: Parker is explaining net metering policy history — this is closer-tier content. The customer asked about her friends' $8 bills and Dominion buyback. The correct setter move is a brief acknowledgment and a bridge to the specialist: 'Net metering rules vary by utility and have changed — that's exactly what our specialist walks through with the actual numbers for your home.' Instead Parker spent approximately 15 seconds on policy history that (a) he may not have right, (b) is not his job to explain, and (c) did not resolve the customer's underlying concern.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker was trying to manage a specific expectation (the $8 bill) before it became a post-appointment disappointment. Reasonable intent, but the execution crossed into scope creep and the expectation was not actually managed — the customer's next question was still about savings percentages.
coaching_implication: This is a short, clean scope-creep example to use in a 1-on-1. Ask Parker: what was your goal in explaining net metering history? Then show him the universal escape hatch from the codex and ask him to rephrase the moment using it.

quote: And it's a new construction in 2023.
stewart_read: New construction in 2023 means the roof is approximately two years old — well within the viable range for solar installation and likely under builder warranty. This is a strong qualification signal that Parker did not anchor on. He responded by looking at satellite imagery and commenting on the neighborhood, which is rapport-adjacent but not value-anchored. The correct move is to name the advantage explicitly: a two-year-old roof is ideal timing for solar because the roof won't need replacement before the system pays back.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have been using the satellite imagery check as a natural transition. The enthusiasm signal was not missed in terms of qualification — he did not DQ the lead — but the opportunity to build excitement around the timing advantage was walked past.
coaching_implication: Show Parker this moment as an example of an enthusiasm signal that could have been anchored. Suggested intent: 'A 2023 roof is actually ideal — you won't need to worry about re-roofing before the system pays back, which is one of the first things our specialist checks.' This also surfaces the roof_type_age verify item, which Parker never explicitly completed.

quote: Really, the last thing now is just a good day to kinda show you that design, Tracy.
stewart_read: Parker uses the name 'Tracy' — not confirmed anywhere in the transcript. The customer's name was never captured during verify. Parker confirmed address, homeowner status, single-family home, and residence length, but the customer's name was never established. Moving to the close with an unconfirmed name is a verify gap that compounds into a rapport risk.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have had the name from the CRM record and was using it from the lead data. Possible, but he then uses 'Trish' at 10:04 — two different names — which rules out confident CRM-sourced usage.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 10:04 moment. Ask Parker: what is this customer's name? He will not be able to answer from memory. That is the coaching point — name confirmation is a verify item, not a CRM assumption. The closer is walking into an appointment where the customer's name is unknown.

quote: And then we'll plan to drop by just tomorrow at eleven, kinda run through that design.
stewart_read: Parker frames the appointment as an in-person visit ('drop by'). The codex requires: phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, and 'is that fair' verbal handshake. None of these appear. If the closer is expecting a phone appointment and the customer is expecting someone at her door, the appointment fails before it starts. The 'is that fair' omission removes the verbal commitment that predicts show rate.
alternative_hypothesis: Ion may run in-person closer visits in this market, in which case 'drop by' is correct. However, the codex specifies phone_appointment_specified as a required button-up element, and the no-driving/computer anchor and 'is that fair' handshake are required regardless of format.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through the button_up checklist against this call. He hit appointment datetime and one-hour block. He missed phone-vs-in-person clarification, the computer anchor, and the verbal handshake. Ask him: if the customer is driving at 11AM tomorrow and the closer calls, what happens? That is the no-show scenario the button-up protocol prevents.

quote: Well, thanks for your time, Trish.
stewart_read: Parker closes with 'Trish' after using 'Tracy' at 08:25. Two different names, neither confirmed in the transcript. This is not a minor slip — the customer's name is the most basic verify item, and the closer will address her by whatever name is in the handoff. If the closer uses 'Tracy' or 'Trish' and neither is correct, the rapport damage lands at the top of the appointment.
coaching_implication: Use this as the anchor for a verify discipline conversation. The name error is visible, concrete, and easy to replay. Ask Parker: at what point in the call did you confirm her name? Then show him the verify checklist. The name gap is the symptom; the coaching target is verify completion as a habit, not a checkbox.
